behaviors.description: Cell containing =SUM(A1:A10) evaluates to the correct sum of values in the range
behaviors.justification: Range references require parsing range syntax (A1:A10), iterating over cells, and collecting their values. A pure in-process mock of cell lookup would hide bugs in range boundary calculation, range-to-cell iteration, or incomplete cell collection. Real cell objects in a worksheet are necessary.
behaviors.test_description: Create worksheet with cells A1:A10 containing values 1–10. Set cell B1 to formula '=SUM(A1:A10)'. Evaluate. Assert B1 equals 55. Test edge cases: empty cells in range, single cell range, range boundaries.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Self-challenge: Could a Unit test with mocked cell values miss this? Yes—if range parsing breaks (e.g., treating A1:A10 as a single cell lookup), a test that mocks get_cell_value(ref) for individual cells wouldn't catch it. Integration level is necessary.

behaviors.description: Cell referencing another formula cell (e.g., C1=B1+1 where B1=A1*2) produces correct cascaded result
behaviors.justification: Tests dependency resolution and evaluation order. Cell C1 must use B1's computed value, and B1 must compute before C1. Mocking individual cell evaluations would hide bugs in topological ordering or failure to propagate updates downstream.
behaviors.test_description: Set A1=5, B1='=A1*2', C1='=B1+1'. Evaluate. Assert C1=11. Change A1 to 10. Assert C1 recomputes to 21 (not retaining old B1). Verify A1→B1→C1 dependency chain works in both directions.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A Unit test of C1's formula in isolation (with B1 pre-evaluated to 10) would pass even if the evaluator skips B1 during C1's evaluation. Only Integration with the full dependency graph catches evaluation-order bugs.

behaviors.description: Circular dependency (A1=B1, B1=A1) is detected and returns error instead of infinite loop/stack overflow
behaviors.justification: Must verify that the engine detects cycles and returns an error without hanging or crashing. Cycle detection could be a standalone algorithm or embedded in the evaluator. To ensure it works in the evaluation pipeline and prevents infinite recursion, set up circular formulas and verify they don't hang.
behaviors.test_description: Set A1='=B1', B1='=A1'. Attempt to evaluate either cell. Assert that evaluation completes (doesn't hang) and both cells return a #CIRC (circular reference) error, not an exception or timeout.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A Unit test of just the cycle-detection algorithm with graph input would catch a broken algorithm. But it would miss if the algorithm is never called during evaluation, or if it's called too late (after recursion has begun). Integration level testing the full evaluation pipeline is necessary.

behaviors.description: VLOOKUP with exact match returns correct value; returns error when key is not found
behaviors.justification: VLOOKUP searches a table range and returns a column value. Requires real lookup table structure in cells. Mocking the table data would hide bugs in column offset calculation, exact-match logic, or handling edge cases like duplicate keys.
behaviors.test_description: Set up lookup table (A1:B5: names in A, values in B). Set D1 to a name. Cell C1='=VLOOKUP(D1,A1:B5,2,FALSE)'. Assert C1 returns correct value. Set D1 to a non-existent name. Assert C1 returns #N/A error. Test: duplicate keys in column A (verify first match), boundary cells (A1, B5).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test of VLOOKUP logic with pre-constructed table arrays could verify the search algorithm, but would miss bugs in how range A1:B5 is parsed, evaluated, or iterated. Integration with real cells catches these.

behaviors.justification: IF logic is local to expression evaluation—branch on condition, evaluate selected branch. The cell reference A1 can be mocked with a value. Mocking wouldn't hide the nested branching logic, which is self-contained.
behaviors.test_description: Parametrized test with cases: (A1=5, expect 'low'), (A1=15, expect 'medium'), (A1=25, expect 'high'). Mock A1 value. Evaluate formula. Verify correct branch. Test deeply nested IFs (3+ levels).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The behavior is about nested conditional logic, not about how A1 is resolved. A Unit test with mocked A1 value would catch bugs in nested IF evaluation (wrong condition, wrong branch) because those bugs are in the IF logic itself.

behaviors.description: CONCATENATE joins cell values with correct separator and skips empty cells (doesn't insert blank strings)
behaviors.justification: CONCATENATE logic is local—iterate arguments, skip empty, join. Cell references can be mocked with values. The behavior is about filtering and joining, not about cell storage or lookup.
behaviors.test_description: Unit tests with mocked cell values: (A1='Hello', A2='', A3='World') → 'Hello-World' (not 'Hello--World'). (A1='', A2='', A3='') → ''. (A1='X', A2='', A3='Y', separator='-') → 'X-Y'. Verify empty-cell detection logic.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Mocking A1, A2, A3 as values is sufficient because the behavior is about the concatenation and filtering logic, not about resolving cell references. A Unit test would catch bugs in empty-check or join logic.

behaviors.description: Formula referencing a different sheet (=Sheet2!A1+1) resolves correctly when referenced sheet exists
behaviors.justification: Requires resolving a sheet name, finding another worksheet object, and looking up a cell in it. Mocking a single sheet would hide bugs in sheet lookup, qualified reference parsing, or cross-sheet cell resolution.
behaviors.test_description: Create two worksheets: Sheet1 and Sheet2. Set Sheet2.A1=10. In Sheet1.B1, set formula '=Sheet2!A1+1'. Evaluate. Assert B1=11. Test error: reference non-existent sheet → #REF! error. Test: reference non-existent cell → #REF! error.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Multi-sheet state is necessary. A Unit test with a mocked sheet lookup would miss bugs in how the qualified reference syntax is parsed or how the referenced sheet's cells are accessed.

behaviors.description: When a referenced cell's value changes, all dependent formulas recalculate in correct topological order
behaviors.justification: Tests calculation order in a dependency DAG. Changing A1 must trigger B1 before C1 before D1. Mocking individual recalculations would hide bugs in the dependency graph construction or traversal order—a naive implementation might update C1 before B1, giving stale results.
behaviors.test_description: Set A1=1, B1='=A1*2', C1='=B1+3', D1='=C1*2'. Change A1 to 10. Verify recalculation order (use a spy/mock to log calls) and final values: B1=20, C1=23, D1=46. Test with longer chains, diamond dependencies (two cells both depend on A1).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A Unit test spying on recalculation call order might catch a wrong topological sort. But it would miss bugs where cells are recalculated in the right order but with stale values (e.g., C1 uses old B1 value). Integration level with real cell values ensures correctness.

behaviors.description: Undo after formula edit restores formula text and all previously computed dependent values
behaviors.justification: Must verify state restoration across multiple interdependent cells. Mocking the undo stack would hide bugs in what state is captured/restored. Need real cell objects and undo mechanism.
behaviors.test_description: Set A1=10, B1='=A1*2' (value 20), C1='=B1+5' (value 25). Edit B1 to '=A1*3' (value 30, C1 becomes 35). Call undo. Assert B1 formula restored to '=A1*2', B1 value=20, C1 value=25 (not 35). Test multiple edits and undos.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Undo is state management across a dependency graph. A Unit test with mocked undo stack would verify the undo mechanism in isolation but miss bugs where dependent cells' values aren't captured in undo state. Integration is necessary.

behaviors.description: Saving workbook to disk and loading produces identical cell values, formulas, and floating-point precision (no data loss)
behaviors.justification: Involves real filesystem I/O and serialization format. Could miss bugs in: format encoding, float-to-string/string-to-float conversion, deserialization logic, file paths, corruption. In-memory mock wouldn't expose floating-point rounding or file-level issues.
behaviors.test_description: Create workbook: A1=3.14159265359 (high precision), B1='=A1*2', C1='=SUM(A1:B1)'. Save to file. Load into new engine. Assert A1 value bit-exact, B1 formula exact string, C1 matches original. Test: special values (±Inf, NaN), empty cells, large numbers, formulas with special chars.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Only real I/O catches serialization format bugs, byte-order issues, or floating-point precision loss during round-trip conversion. An in-memory mock would hide these failures.
